title: Breakers Roar
original_prose:
“In the dying light / You can drag me through hell / If it meant I could hold your hand”— Sturgill Simpson, “Breakers Roar”

####

[PRIORITY-CLASS STRATEGIC RESPONSE | ORIGIN: CENTRAL STRATEGIC AUTHORITY]TO: ALL VESSEL COMPLIANCE DIVISIONS | ALL WRAITH DIVISIONS | CORE PERIMETER CONTROLSUBJECT: BAPTISTE-LAVEAU, MADELEINE JOSETTE – STATUS: NONCOMPLIANT ICON / ACTIVE INSURRECTION VECTOR

Effective immediately, the individual known as Baptiste-Laveau, alias "Kat," is to be apprehended using all available resources. Tactical escalation beyond Standard Yield Thresholds is authorized.

Context: Following the incident at SYNRGY ONE (Las Vegas), unauthorized footage of Subject Kat neutralizing multiple WRAITH-class vessels atop a waste transport has propagated through multiple noncompliant nodes. The act, coupled with Subject's identity presentation and associated mythologizing, has catalyzed unprecedented Signal disorder in formerly stabilized sectors.

Gridpoint MOR-12 reported student walkouts and Spark denial acts across six institutions.

Gridpoint ATL-07 reports spontaneous unrest in multiple containment zones.

Four WRAITH units (Beta class) suffered autonomous refusal-to-engage failures in direct proximity to civilians repeating variations of Subject’s vocal patterns.

CoreSync deviations at 0.4% above tolerance across 29 active vessels.

Directive:

The Subject is not to be converted. No Spark extraction. Capture for live Signal mapping and termination.

Do not kill. Do not negotiate. Do not engage outside structured Protocol.

Psychological resonance tests confirm: Subject Baptiste-Laveau displays elevated disruption indices in proximity to key martyr algorithms.

The Subject is not a person. It is a vector. Treat accordingly.

Concerning Lieutenant Blake (Alpha-32):

Telemetry traces remain partially active. Utilize with caution. Full behavioral fracture not yet confirmed.

Do not attempt retrieval. Observe only. Proximity to Subject Kat renders Alpha-32's compliance null.

Additional Measures:

All footage, textual references, or mnemonic echoes of the Las Vegas incident must be scrubbed from SynRgyNet within one cycle.

Spread or discussion of said events now classified as Class One Noncompliance.

Punishment: Immediate Spark Harvest. No appeals. No correctional delay.

Remember: Faith is structure. Thought is noise. The Protocol does not fail.

— EXEC. A. VOSS / STRATEGIC AUTHORITY / VERIFIED CORE ENACTMENT



####

The city burned behind us. But not loud. Not bright. Not like it should’ve. SynRgy One had been a beacon once. Now it just smoldered in the rearview—slow and quiet, like even the fire had given up on making a statement.

Kat didn’t speak.

She just drove. Hands loose on the wheel. Eyes forward. Mouth a flat line like she’d used up all her words in Vegas and didn’t have any left to waste.

The highway cracked in places. Not from bombs. From heat. Asphalt buckled like old skin. We passed a tractor-trailer melted into its own shadow, metal liquefied and re-fused in the shape of surrender. A cattle fence twisted up through the median like wire pulled from a corpse.

The sun was already brutal. Not hot yet, but angry. The kind of sky that meant lightning later. Dry strikes. Flash floods. Storms with names.

Ahead, a dust devil spun itself into a frenzy, then vanished mid-twirl like it remembered it had somewhere else to be.

I tried to say something. Nothing brave. Just… something. But it caught in my throat. Like the words knew they weren’t welcome.

The sword lay across the back seat. Wrapped in a motel blanket. Still humming.

I'd pulled the tracking unit out from under the dash twenty minutes after we left the gates. Smashed it with the butt of my rifle, tossed it in the median. Probably still blinking somewhere near a burned-out welcome sign. Whatever signal SynRgy had on us, it ended there.

They’d have to track us the old way now. Visually. Pattern prediction. Thermal ghosts and prayer. The link was severed. Not loud. Just there. A pulse. A reminder. Kat hadn’t touched it since we fled. Neither had I.

We drove.

The road narrowed into silence. No cars. No checkpoints. No drones, for now. Just the sky holding its breath.

I glanced sideways. She didn’t meet my eyes.

Just said, softly: “Keep your hands where they are. They’re shakin’.”

I looked down. She was right.

I pressed them flat against my thighs.

“Sorry.”

She didn’t answer. Didn’t nod. Didn’t need to.

Somewhere behind us, a WRAITH would find our tire tracks. But not yet. Not for a few more hours. And not until the story finished telling itself.

A flash lit the horizon—lightning, but no thunder. A dry flicker. False storm. The kind the desert learned to fake when real weather stopped making sense.

Kat finally exhaled. Not loud. But deep.

She lit a cigarette without asking. Cracked the window. The smoke whipped sideways and vanished behind us.

Still, she didn’t speak.

But the silence had changed.

Something in it was loading.

A truth with her name on it.

And I knew—when she was ready to say it, I wouldn’t interrupt. Not once.



####

She spoke without warning. No setup. No clearing of the throat. Just a question dropped into the dust between us.

“You ever heard of two-spirit?”

I blinked. “Yeah. I think. A little.”

She didn’t scoff. Just drove.

“My mama said it like it was a birthright. Said the old spirits still made people like me. ‘Cause sometimes the world needs someone who can walk both roads. Not just one.”

I waited.

She didn’t look over. Eyes on the road. Voice even.

“We lived in a trailer not far from Houma. Floodplain. Mold in the corners, rust in the pipes. Mama called it home. Said the land knew our names. We had a dock out back that leaned like an old drunk, but she made me sit on it every morning. Said I had to listen before I could speak.”

She smiled then. Just a flicker. Like a scar that remembered how it got there.

“She didn’t trust the government. Or the church. Or anyone who didn’t eat with their hands. She taught me prayers in Kouri-Vini, half-forgotten songs she swore the river gave her. Told me if I listened long enough, I’d hear it speak back.”

I swallowed. Said nothing.

“She knew what I was. Before I did. Never called me wrong. Never told me to pick a side. Just said, ‘You got both fire and water in you, bébé. That’s a dangerous blessing.’”

She flicked the ash out the cracked window.

“My daddy took longer. He wasn’t cruel, just… afraid. Of what folks would say. Of what it meant. He called me his boy till I hit thirteen. Then I got in a fight at school. Boy twice my size. Called me a halfbreed faggot. I split his lip and gave him a black eye before the teacher even stood up.”

A pause. Not dramatic. Just real.

“Daddy picked me up from school and didn’t say a word all the way home. Next morning, he left a dress on my bed. One of mama’s old ones, altered with a belt. Said, ‘If you’re gonna fight like a Baptiste, you might as well do it in armor.’ Never called me his son again.”

The road unspooled ahead of us. Long and cracked and straight.

“I didn’t have the language yet. Not ‘til I found it in a book some rez librarian scanned and saved on a flash drive. Said two-spirit was the old way of naming people like me. Said we used to be sacred.”

She glanced over, just once.

“Then the church showed up.”

I didn’t know what to say. So I said the truth.

“I don’t think I ever knew anyone like you.”

She raised an eyebrow. Not cruel. Just sharp.

“You did. You just didn’t know it.”

That hit.

Not like a slap. Like a key.

“I trained with soldiers who wore lipstick in secret. Got drunk with techs who unzipped their uniforms like it might unzip their skin. I saw one man hang himself in a barracks latrine ‘cause they caught him humming along to an old Brandi Carlile track.”

I rubbed my palms down my thighs again. Couldn’t stop.

She nodded, slow. “Yeah. You knew us.”

She didn’t smile again. Not this time. Just drove.

“There was this oak tree behind our trailer,” she said after a while. “Had a scar across the bark where lightning hit it back in ’96. Mama said it marked the land as chosen. I used to sit under it and ask the wind why I felt like a lie. Wind never answered. But it never left me, either.”

The sky ahead crackled again. More lightning. No thunder. Still no rain.

“By the time I was fifteen, I had a name for it. Josette. Mine. Not a disguise. Not a phase. Just the truth I couldn’t not speak anymore.”

I didn’t interrupt.

She went on:

“First person I told was my aunt. She cried. Said she already had a niece. Didn’t need another. I told her I wasn’t another. Just the one I always was.”

She laughed. It sounded more like smoke than joy.

“She stopped coming around after that.”

Another long silence. But it didn’t feel empty.

“People talk about how brave it is to come out,” she said. “But I wasn’t trying to be brave. I was just tryin’ to stop bleeding.”

We passed a dead gas station. Pumps yanked out. Windows bricked up. Someone had spray-painted GOD LEFT across the awning.

“She stayed, though,” Kat said. “Mama. Even when I started getting stares in town. Even when the preacher stopped making eye contact. Even when the sheriff started ‘randomly checking’ our power hookup every week. She stayed.”

I wanted to ask what happened to her.

I didn’t.

Instead I said, “She sounds like the kind of woman who didn’t take shit from anybody.”

Kat’s voice dropped. “She was.”

We didn’t speak again for another ten miles.

Then:

“She got sick. Not cancer. Something slower. Brain fog. Shakes. Docs said it was environmental. But I knew. Knew it was the grid. Knew SynRgy poisoned the water when they rerouted the overflow into our bayou. Didn’t even lie about it. Just said it was ‘necessary for optimized Core yield.’”

She lit another cigarette. Didn’t offer me one.

“When she died, they didn’t let me bury her out back. Said the land was owned. Said burial was wasteful. Against policy. I burned her under the oak instead. Didn’t ask. Just did it. Called the flames by their names.”

I couldn’t speak. I just… couldn’t.

But I remember what she said next. Clear as anything.

“I didn’t become a rebel when they shot at me. Or when they took my license. Or when I lost my job. I became a rebel the day they told me I wasn’t allowed to remember her the way she deserved.”

She dropped the cigarette out the window.

“I remember everything. That’s why they hate me.”



####

The gas station looked dead. Like everything else. No signage, no brand. Just a box of blistered cinderblock on the edge of a highway that didn’t belong to anyone anymore.

Kat slowed. Pulled in without asking. Dust rose in lazy spirals behind us, then settled like ash.

The pump coughed when she squeezed the handle. Once. Twice. Then gave in. The numbers flicked slowly. Old-school. Analog. SynRgy must’ve forgotten this place.

I stepped inside.

Cooler in the corner hummed too loud. The bottles inside looked too clean. Labels unpeeled. No dust. Someone was restocking. Or someone was watching.

The clerk stood behind the counter. Mid-forties. SynRgy-fit. Shirt tucked. Hands folded just a little too still.

He smiled.

"Morning."

I nodded. Didn’t answer.

Kat came in behind me. Didn’t speak either. Just walked to the smokes, grabbed a pack, and dropped it on the counter.

“Six even,” he said. But didn’t reach for the register.

She slid him a coin. He took it. Never looked down.

His eyes flicked to my side. Just a glance. Just long enough to register the shape of the sword beneath the blanket in my hand.

“Long drive?” he asked.

Kat stared.

He smiled again. “Storms coming up near the canyon. Roads wash out fast. You two be careful.”

She didn’t say thank you. Just nodded once and walked out.

I followed.

The pump clicked off. She didn’t top off. Just enough to keep moving.

We were back on the road in under two minutes.

“He was too friendly,” I said.

“Yup.”

“You think he flagged us?”

“He didn’t have to,” she said. “This whole stretch of road is wired. He just logged the anomaly and the system took care of the rest.”

We hit sixty. Then seventy. No more talking.

Then I heard it.

Soft.

A high-pitched whir, like an old television left on in another room.

I looked up. Sky was clear—except for one speck moving against the sun.

A drone.

Not low enough to be scouting. Not high enough to be passive.

It was watching.

Logging.

Kat didn’t look up. Didn’t flinch.

“They’re not coming yet,” she said. “That’s just confirmation.”

“Confirmation of what?”

She lit a cigarette.

“That we’re still alive.”



####

She broke the silence again. Like she was peeling something off.

“I used to teach.”

I didn’t answer. Just watched the road.

“Middle school. New Orleans. Charter campus. Soundproof doors. Biometric attendance. Cameras in the ceiling, but I knew where the blind spots were.”

She tapped the steering wheel once, twice. Like it helped her remember.

“They let us teach history, long as it was cleared. Standardized. Sanitized. They wanted names and dates. Victory metrics. No context. No grief.”

She smiled. Not like she meant it.

“So I made a game of it. Built shadow lessons. Taught what they didn’t say, in the gaps between what they did. Showed my kids how to read a curriculum sideways. How to ask the questions that got erased.”

Her voice had rhythm now. Not soft. But sure.

“I let them rename themselves. Every year. Said names had power, and the ones we got handed didn’t always fit. One year I had a class of nothing but gods and ghosts and made-up animals. One kid called herself Junebug the Destroyer. Wore paper horns and refused to sit still. I let her.”

I felt something in my throat. Didn’t speak.

“They called it subversion,” she said. “When they found out. That was the word they used. Subversion of educational objectives. Abuse of Spark trust.”

She reached for a bottle of water. Drank. Tossed it out the window.

“But it wasn’t just that. The thing that broke it wasn’t me.”

We passed a cluster of burned-out homes. One still had a plastic trike in the yard. Melted into the grass.

“It was lunch. Third period. Just another Tuesday. I was walking the perimeter—habit, not policy. That’s when I saw the Harvest van.”

Her hands tightened on the wheel. Just for a second.

“They came for Javi. Sweet kid. Quiet. Drew maps of cities that didn’t exist. Called himself a cartographer of possible futures. I knew he was Spark-strong. You could feel it around him. That kind of gravity isn’t subtle.”

The sky ahead was beginning to pinken. But there was no beauty in it.

“No warning. No consultation. They just scanned him at the lunch line. Red light blinked twice. They bagged him like he was a stray animal. Didn’t even let him drop his tray.”

My hands curled. I didn’t realize it until the nails dug in.

“I ran. Tried to stop it. One of the guards hit me with a neural prod. Right in the hip. Dropped me in front of fifty kids. They all watched.”

She rubbed her leg.

“Still twinges sometimes. When the weather turns.”

A pause.

“They suspended me for interference. Said I broke compliance protocol. Asked me to sign a reaffirmation oath before returning. I told them to eat shit.”

“Then they fired you?”

“No. They just… stopped paying me. No termination notice. Just silence. Like if they didn’t name it, it wouldn’t count.”

“And Echo?”

“Found me in a library two towns over. I was camped out in a solar kiosk, reading bootleg PDFs of pre-Collapse pedagogy. They slid a folder across the table and said, ‘We saw what you did with your students. You want to do it louder?’”

She glanced at me.

“I didn’t hesitate.”

I tried to imagine her in a classroom. Couldn’t. Not because it didn’t fit—because it fit too well. Made everything else harder to stomach.

“What happened to the rest of the kids?”

She didn’t answer right away.

Finally: “Some vanished. Some got re-educated. A few found me later. They called me Miss Kat. Like it was still my name.”

“Is it?”

She didn’t look away from the road.

“No. But it’s theirs. And I don’t mind carrying it for a while longer.”



####

We crested a hill and the world changed.

A town lay below us, tucked like a secret into a dry basin—no name on the road signs, no movement, no smoke. Just the hollow geometry of abandonment. But it hadn’t been abandoned long.

The doors were all open. Not broken. Just ajar. Like someone meant to come back.

Beds unmade. Curtains breathing the wind. A doll at the foot of a stoop. Still clean.

We slowed.

Then we saw the truck.

SynRgy issue. White. Standard Harvest configuration. Its back doors hung open. Ramps still deployed. No guards. No civilians. Just cages. Empty.

Kat didn’t speak. Just pulled to the side of the road and killed the engine.

We sat in it.

The stillness.

The kind of quiet that screams.

Then she whispered:

“They came in daylight. People watched. No one screamed.”

She opened her door and stepped out. I followed.

We didn’t touch anything. Didn’t have to.

Footprints everywhere. Small ones. Some barefoot. Drag marks by the curb.

A kitchen window had a handprint on the glass—tiny. Smudged by panic.

Inside the truck, someone had scrawled something on the wall in black grease. REMEMBER US.

I turned to her.

Her jaw was set. Not clenched. Just braced.

“I’m sorry,” I said. Quiet. But real.

She didn’t move.

“I mean it. For all of it. I don’t know how else to say it.”

She lit a cigarette. Took a drag. Looked out over the town.

“You didn’t build the cage,” she said. “But you fed it.”

She flicked ash toward the truck. “So did I.”

I stepped back. Let the weight settle.

“I don’t hate you,” she said.

“But I’m not gonna hold your hand through the guilt. That’s your job.”

I nodded.

She didn’t look over. Just said, “Just be quiet. Just remember it right. That’s all I ask.”

We left the town in silence.







####

The road stretched long again.

The sun dipped lower. Shadows got long. The air smelled like rain that never came.

Kat spoke again.

Not a story this time. A memory wrapped in something older.

“My mama never called it religion,” she said. “Said that word belonged to men with pens and pulpits. Said what we had was older. A pact. Between bone and river.”

She glanced over at me. “You ever been baptized in swampwater?”

I shook my head.

She smiled. “It ain’t clean. Ain’t sterile. You come up with mud in your mouth and bugs in your hair. But you feel seen.”

She drove with one hand now. The other rested on her thigh, fingers tapping a slow rhythm.

“She said the river holds memory. That it carries every name ever spoken into it. That’s why the dead float downstream—they’re headed toward home.”

We passed another broken sign. Shot full of holes. It used to say HOLINESS TABERNACLE.

“She brought me out there one night. Said the stars were right. Said the wind had been whispering. Said I was ready.”

I didn’t speak.

“She painted me in ash and honey. Lit a fire and sang in a tongue I never learned. And then she dunked me under. Held me down until the fear turned into something else.”

“What?” I asked.

“Joy,” she said.

She smiled again. For real this time.

“I felt my Spark hum in me. Like it recognized the mud. Like it sang back.”

The road curved. We followed it.

“That’s when I knew,” she said. “I wasn’t a mistake.”

I didn’t answer. Just watched her.

She didn’t need my belief.

She just needed to say it out loud.





####

We crested another ridge.

Red Rocks shimmered in the distance. Glowing like a wound that refused to scar.

I smiled. “We made it.”

Kat didn’t.

Her face was unreadable. Not fear. Not relief. Something colder.

“They let us leave,” she said. “Doesn’t mean they won’t catch up.”

####

The void is smaller again. Just a sliver of rock beneath us now. Enough for him to sit. Barefoot. Still.

He doesn’t look at me right away. Just watches the dark flicker like it's trying to remember what light used to feel like.

Then:

“What does it mean?”

I blink. “What?”

“Two-spirit.”

His voice isn’t judgment. Just gravity. Like he wants to understand before the world ends.

I open my mouth. Close it again.

“It’s not... a thing you can explain in a sentence.”

He waits.

“It’s... it’s someone who walks more than one road. Not a man. Not a woman. Or maybe both. Or maybe something else entirely. It’s not about the body. Not really. It’s older than that.”

He tilts his head. “Then why did they hate her?”

That lands like a blade.

I look down at the sword. Still wrapped. Still humming.

“I don’t know.”

I say it honest. Like a wound.

“I’ve tried to figure it out. I’ve tried to trace the wires back to the first spark of it. The fear. The violence. But it’s... everywhere. Woven into systems. Into stories. Into gods. Like someone drew a line in the dirt a thousand years ago and said ‘you stand here, you don’t.’”

The child doesn’t interrupt. He just watches.

“They called her a lie because she didn’t fit the diagram. Because she made them question what the diagram was even for. That kind of truth scares people. It always has.”

He nods. Just once. “But she wasn’t a lie.”

“No,” I say. My voice cracks. “She was the only true thing I ever met.”

We sit in the silence that follows.

Not because we’ve run out of words.

Because the void has.

He watches me. Not blinking. Not accusing. Just... patient.

Then:

“What am I?”

That one hits harder.

“I thought you were a boy,” I say, slow.

“Is that what you want me to be?”

The question isn’t hostile. It’s clear. Like cold water.

“No,” I say. And then: “It doesn’t matter what I want you to be. It only matters what you want to be.”

He nods. But he’s not done.

“You didn’t know that before Kat, did you?”

I shake my head.

“No.”

The cracks beneath us are deeper now. The sword hums once. Then again. Louder.

I close my eyes.

And try to remember her right.
